Singapore · Civic District · Self-guided family walk

The Forbidden Hill Line

One hill, one river, seven hundred years of stories. A walking route from the famous Fort Canning Tree Tunnel down through old Government Hill to the Singapore River — with spotter challenges for the kids at every stop.

≈ 3 km2–3 hours with stops10 stationsFinish: lunch at Clarke Quay
Start the walk ↓

Route map — tap a station to jump

01Tree Tunnel
02Government Hill
03Spice Garden
04Fort Gate
05Battlebox / WWII
06National Gallery
07City Hall
08The Padang
09Singapore River
10Clarke Quay

Before Singapore had skyscrapers, it had a hill. The Malays called it Bukit Larangan — the Forbidden Hill — because it was believed to be the resting place of ancient kings, and nobody dared climb it. Then Stamford Raffles arrived in 1819, looked at the view, and built his house right on top.

This walk starts on that hill and ends by the river that made the city rich. Along the way: a secret WWII bunker, a garden full of things you can smell, a Gothic gate to a fort that never fired a shot in anger, and two of the grandest buildings in Asia. Each stop has a Spotter Challenge — first one to complete it chooses the ice cream flavour at the end.

01

Start here · Come early!

Fort Canning Tree Tunnel

This is one of the most photographed spots in the whole of Singapore. Stand at the bottom of the spiral staircase and look straight up: a perfect circle of sky, framed by the umbrella-shaped crown of a rain tree. It makes a brilliant group photo — but everybody knows it, so a queue forms from about 9 am. Go early and you'll have it to yourselves.

Spotter challenge

Count the steps as you climb the spiral. Bonus point: the tree above you is called a Rain Tree because its leaves fold up when rain is coming. Are they open or closed today?

📸 Best light: 9–10 am, when silhouettes hit the wall 📍 Open Fort Canning Tree Tunnel in Google Maps
Next stop →02 · Government Hill
02

Up the hill

Fort Canning Park — the old Government Hill

You're now walking on the most important hill in Singapore's history. In the 14th century this was probably the site of a royal palace — archaeologists have dug up 700-year-old gold jewellery here. Later, Stamford Raffles built his bungalow on the summit, and the hill became Government Hill, home to Singapore's colonial governors, before the fort was built in the 1850s.

Spotter challenge

This hill has been renamed at least four times: Forbidden Hill, Singapore Hill, Government Hill, Fort Canning. Which name do you think suits it best — and what would you call it?

⛏️ Gold ornaments from the 1300s were dug up here in 1928 📍 Open Fort Canning Park in Google Maps
← Previous01 · Tree TunnelNext stop →03 · Spice Garden
03

Use your nose

The Spice Garden

Raffles wasn't just an empire-builder — he was a plant nerd. In 1822 he set aside land here for Singapore's very first botanical garden, hoping to turn the island into a major spice-growing centre. The early garden included nutmeg and cloves, and helped kick off Singapore's spice plantation era. Today's Spice Garden recreates it with over 180 herbs and spices.

Spotter challenge — the smell test

Find and gently smell (don't pick!) three of these: nutmeg, clove, cinnamon, laksa leaf, torch ginger. Which one smells most like something we eat at home?

🌿 The 1822 garden started with 125 nutmeg and 450 clove trees 📍 Open Spice Garden in Google Maps
← Previous02 · Government HillNext stop →04 · Fort Gate
04

The last piece of the fort

Fort Gate

In 1859 the hill switched jobs: the governor's house came down, and a military fort went up, with thick walls, barracks and seven big cannons pointing at the sea. There was just one problem — enemy ships could bombard the town while staying out of range of the fort's guns! The fort was demolished in 1907 without ever firing a shot in battle. This heavy gate and a couple of nine-pound cannons are nearly all that's left.

Spotter challenge

A cannon was fired from this fort at 5 am every morning as the whole town's alarm clock. Would you rather be woken by a cannon or by Dad's phone alarm? Find the old cannons nearby and count them.

💣 Built 1859–61 · demolished 1907 · never fired a shot in battle 📍 Open Fort Gate in Google Maps
← Previous03 · Spice GardenNext stop →05 · Battlebox
05

A serious moment

The Battlebox & the Fall of Singapore

Deep beneath this hill is a secret bunker with metre-thick concrete walls: the Headquarters Malaya Command Operations Bunker, better known as the Battlebox. It was here, on the morning of 15 February 1942, that Lieutenant-General Arthur Percival and his commanders made the painful decision to surrender Singapore to the Japanese — one of the most dramatic moments of World War II in Asia. (The surrender itself was signed later that day at the old Ford Factory in Bukit Timah.) After the war the bunker was sealed and its location forgotten, until it was rediscovered in 1988. Today it's a museum you can tour.

Spotter challenge

The bunker is hidden about 9 metres below the hill and had more than twenty rooms — including its own telephone exchange. If you had to hide underground for days, what three things would you bring?

🗓️ 15 February 1942 — the decision to surrender was made right here 📍 Open Battlebox in Google Maps
← Previous04 · Fort GateNext stop →06 · National Gallery
↓

LEAVE THE HILL · Walk downhill towards Coleman Street and cross into the Civic District — the grand white buildings ahead are your next stop.

06

Two buildings, one gallery

National Gallery Singapore

These two giants — the former Supreme Court (spot the green dome) and City Hall — have been joined together into one enormous museum. Inside is the largest public collection of modern Singapore and Southeast Asian art in the world. Even if you don't go in, walk close: the Corinthian columns and the sculpture in the Supreme Court's triangular pediment were made by an Italian sculptor, Rodolfo Nolli.

Spotter challenge

Count the giant columns along the front of the old Supreme Court. Then find the figure of Justice in the triangle up top — what is she holding?

🎨 Aircon tip: the Gallery basement is a great heat-escape 📍 Open National Gallery in Google Maps
← Previous05 · BattleboxNext stop →07 · City Hall
07

Where a nation grew up

City Hall & the road to independence

City Hall is where history kept happening. The Japanese surrender in Southeast Asia was formally accepted inside this building in September 1945; Singapore's first fully elected government was sworn in here; and its steps have been the front row for parades and proclamations ever since. Turning these buildings into the National Gallery kept all that civic history alive while opening the doors to everyone.

Spotter challenge

Stand on the City Hall steps and look out. You're seeing almost exactly the view that leaders saw at the biggest moments of Singapore's story. Strike your best "important announcement" pose for a photo.

🏛️ 1945: Japanese surrender accepted on these steps 📍 Open City Hall in Google Maps
← Previous06 · National GalleryNext stop →08 · The Padang
08

The big green rectangle

The Padang

Padang simply means "field" in Malay — but this is the field. It has hosted cricket matches since the 1830s, National Day Parades, and victory celebrations. Stand in the middle and turn a slow circle: colonial buildings on one side, gleaming skyscrapers on the other. It's the best single spot to see old and new Singapore facing each other.

Spotter challenge

Old-vs-new count: how many colonial buildings can you spot on one side, and how many skyscrapers on the other? Which side wins?

🏏 Cricket has been played here for nearly 200 years 📍 Open The Padang in Google Maps
← Previous07 · City HallNext stop →09 · Singapore River
09

Change to the River Line

The Singapore River

This river was the working heart of colonial Singapore. Everything the port traded — rubber, rice, spices, tin — moved along this water in flat wooden boats called bumboats or lighters, and the quays were jammed with traders, coolies and warehouses. Imagine the noise and the smell! Today the water is clean and the old shophouses hold restaurants, but the shape of the river hasn't changed.

Spotter challenge

Bumboats traditionally had eyes painted on their bows — sailors believed the eyes helped the boat "see" its way safely. Can you spot a boat with eyes on the river today? Also: count the bridges you can see from one spot.

🚤 Boats with painted eyes still cruise this river 📍 Open Boat Quay in Google Maps
← Previous08 · The PadangNext stop →10 · Clarke Quay
10

Terminus · Lunch!

Clarke Quay

You made it! Clarke Quay was once a stretch of riverside godowns — warehouses where all that river cargo was stored. Today the same buildings, painted in sherbet colours, are packed with restaurants and cafés, which makes this the perfect finish line. Pick a table by the water, order something cold, and watch the bumboats go past exactly where the lighters worked 150 years ago.

Final challenge

Tally up your spotter points from all ten stops. Winner picks the ice cream flavour — and gets to choose where we eat lunch. (Godown fun fact for the table: the word may come from the Malay gudang, meaning warehouse.)

🍜 Route complete: ~3 km, 700 years of history 📍 Open Clarke Quay in Google Maps
← Previous09 · Singapore RiverFinish →Tips & route map

Good to know before you set off

Timing: Start by 8.30–9 am to beat both the Tree Tunnel queue and the midday heat. The hill section is shaded; the Padang and river are not — hats and water bottles for everyone.

Getting there: Start from Dhoby Ghaut MRT (Exit B) for the Tree Tunnel; finish at Clarke Quay MRT, right by lunch.

🗺️ Open the full walking route in Google Maps

Optional extras: The Battlebox bunker runs guided tours if the kids want to go underground, and the National Gallery makes an excellent air-conditioned detour at the halfway point.
